verses.text: Then Jerubbaal, who is Gideon, and all the people that were with him, rose up early, and pitched beside the well of Harod: so that the host of the Midianites were on the north side of them, by the hill of Moreh, in the valley. The LORD said unto Gideon, The people that are with thee are too many for me to give the Midianites into their hands, lest Israel vaunt themselves against me, saying, Mine own hand hath saved me. Now therefore go to, proclaim in the ears of the people, saying, Whosoever is fearful and afraid, let him return and depart early from mount Gilead. There returned of the people twenty and two thousand; and there remained ten thousand. Guidance: The well is named 'Harod' — 'trembling.' The water at the staging ground is already prophetically named. Then God's astonishing problem: 'too many.' Numerically the Midianites outnumber Israel four to one; theologically Israel outnumbers what God can use without being stolen credit. Permission to leave is granted; over two-thirds walk away. The narrator does not shame the 22,000; God Himself made the offer. Faith does not flatter itself about how many will stay when the door is opened.

The LORD said unto Gideon, The people are yet too many; bring them down unto the water, and I will try them for thee there: and it shall be, that of whom I say unto thee, This shall go with thee, the same shall go with thee; and of whomsoever I say unto thee, This shall not go with thee, the same shall not go. So he brought down the people unto the water: and the LORD said unto Gideon, Every one that lappeth of the water with his tongue, as a dog lappeth, him shalt thou set by himself; likewise every one that boweth down upon his knees to drink. The number of them that lapped, putting their hand to their mouth, were three hundred men: but all the rest of the people bowed down upon their knees to drink water. The LORD said unto Gideon, By the three hundred men that lapped will I save you, and deliver the Midianites into thine hand: and let all the other people go every man unto his place. Guidance: A second cut, and a stranger one. The criterion looks arbitrary on purpose: how you drink water. The point is not who is the better soldier (the 300 who lapped were not necessarily more alert; kneeling to drink was the normal way). The point is that the criterion is the LORD's choice, not human merit. Even Gideon does not understand the selection — only that God has done the choosing. The 9,700 who go home were not failures; they were excess. God's calculus does not need them today.

So the people took victuals in their hand, and their trumpets: and he sent all the rest of Israel every man unto his tent, and retained those three hundred men: and the host of Midian was beneath him in the valley. Guidance: The 300 keep the trumpets — apparently every soldier had taken one as standard kit. That detail will become the chapter's whole strategy. Notice the geography: Gideon and 300 men up on the hill; the entire Midianite host down in the valley. Israel can see them by the thousands; they cannot see Israel. The ratio is hopeless; the position is perfect. The LORD chose both.

It came to pass the same night, that the LORD said unto him, Arise, get thee down unto the host; for I have delivered it into thine hand. But if thou fear to go down, go thou with Phurah thy servant down to the host: and thou shalt hear what they say; and afterward shall thine hands be strengthened to go down unto the host. Then went he down with Phurah his servant unto the outside of the armed men that were in the host. Guidance: God meets Gideon's fear before he names it. 'But if thou fear' — no scolding, no shaming. He gives Gideon permission to take a friend and eavesdrop. Spying is sometimes a form of prayer when God arranges what you will overhear. Gideon takes the offer (he's no fool to refuse fortified courage). Phurah's name means 'branch' — Gideon doesn't sneak alone; he goes leaning on someone. Even God's brave men borrow strength from a companion.

The Midianites and the Amalekites and all the children of the east lay along in the valley like grasshoppers for multitude; and their camels were without number, as the sand by the sea side for multitude. When Gideon was come, behold, there was a man that told a dream unto his fellow, and said, Behold, I dreamed a dream, and, lo, a cake of barley bread tumbled into the host of Midian, and came unto a tent, and smote it that it fell, and overturned it, that the tent lay along. His fellow answered and said, This is nothing else save the sword of Gideon son of Joash, a man of Israel: for into his hand hath God delivered Midian, and all the host. Guidance: A poor man's bread (barley, the cheapest grain) crashes into a tent. A rolling loaf of bread takes down a Midianite tent. The dream is comic-grotesque, and yet the second Midianite interprets it correctly on the spot: that is Gideon. The enemy's own fear has already named the outcome by night. God is the playwright; both armies are speaking His script. Note also: the Midianite says 'God hath delivered Midian.' Even the enemy is talking covenant theology in his sleep.

When Gideon heard the telling of the dream, and the interpretation thereof, that he worshipped, and returned into the host of Israel, and said, Arise; for the LORD hath delivered into your hand the host of Midian. He divided the three hundred men into three companies, and he put a trumpet in every man's hand, with empty pitchers, and lamps within the pitchers. Guidance: Gideon hears the dream and does the only right thing — he worships, right there on the edge of the enemy's camp. Worship in the dark, before victory. Then he hurries back to his men with a confidence that wasn't his five minutes earlier. The weapons he picks are absurd: trumpets, clay pots, and torches. No swords mentioned. The fight will be acoustic and visual, not blade-to-blade.

He said unto them, Look on me, and do likewise: and, behold, when I come to the outside of the camp, it shall be that, as I do, so shall ye do. When I blow with a trumpet, I and all that are with me, then blow ye the trumpets also on every side of all the camp, and say, The sword of the LORD, and of Gideon. Guidance: 'Look on me, and do likewise.' Leadership reduced to demonstration: don't take notes, just watch the lead man and copy. The battle cry he gives them: 'The sword of the LORD, and of Gideon.' The LORD's name first; the leader's name second; nothing about the soldiers. The cry every Christian leader since has had to repeat differently — God's name on every banner, your own name absent if possible.

So Gideon, and the hundred men that were with him, came unto the outside of the camp in the beginning of the middle watch; and they had but newly set the watch: and they blew the trumpets, and brake the pitchers that were in their hands. The three companies blew the trumpets, and brake the pitchers, and held the lamps in their left hands, and the trumpets in their right hands to blow withal: and they cried, The sword of the LORD, and of Gideon. They stood every man in his place round about the camp: and all the host ran, and cried, and fled. The three hundred blew the trumpets, and the LORD set every man's sword against his fellow, even throughout all the host: and the host fled to Beth-shittah in Zererath, and to the border of Abel-meholah, unto Tabbath. Guidance: Middle watch — about 10 PM. Fresh sentries just posted; the rest of the army deep asleep. Three hundred men ring the entire encampment, smash their pots, raise their torches, blow their horns, and shout one short cry. From inside the camp, it sounds like an enormous army of trumpeters with a torch each. Midianites stagger out of tents in pitch darkness, see lights on every side, and start swinging at anything that moves. 'The LORD set every man's sword against his fellow' — no Israelite swung a blade. The army defeats itself. The 300's job was noise; God's was killing.

The men of Israel gathered themselves together out of Naphtali, and out of Asher, and out of all Manasseh, and pursued after the Midianites. Gideon sent messengers throughout all mount Ephraim, saying, Come down against the Midianites, and take before them the waters unto Beth-barah and Jordan. Then all the men of Ephraim gathered themselves together, and took the waters unto Beth-barah and Jordan. They took two princes of the Midianites, Oreb and Zeeb; and they slew Oreb upon the rock Oreb, and Zeeb they slew at the winepress of Zeeb: and they pursued Midian, and brought the heads of Oreb and Zeeb to Gideon on the other side Jordan. Guidance: Now the 9,700 who went home and the surrounding tribes finally join in — the chase, not the breakthrough. The 300 broke the army; everyone else got to help finish. Oreb ('raven') and Zeeb ('wolf') — predator names for two princes who become two place-names. The rock and the winepress where they died turn into permanent geographic memorials. Even the small towns will remember. The chapter that began with God thinning the army ends with the whole nation profiting from a victory only the 300 — and the LORD — could deliver.
